title: State v. Carter
summary: Court ruled that extending a traffic stop without additional reasonable suspicion violates the Fourth Amendment.
impact: Officers must clearly articulate new factors before prolonging a stop beyond its original purpose.

title: People v. Ramirez
summary: Vehicle search based solely on nervous behavior was deemed insufficient for probable cause.
impact: Nervousness alone cannot justify a warrantless vehicle search.

title: U.S. v. Holloway
summary: Statements made prior to Miranda warnings were suppressed due to custodial interrogation conditions.
impact: Miranda warnings must be given before questioning when a subject is not free to leave.

title: State v. Nguyen
summary: Court upheld SFST results when administered in compliance with standardized procedures.
impact: Proper SFST administration strengthens DUI prosecution and admissibility.

title: People v. Dawson
summary: Use of force was found reasonable based on totality of circumstances and immediate threat assessment.
impact: Officers must document perceived threat and decision-making factors clearly.

title: U.S. v. Bennett
summary: Search warrant invalidated due to lack of sufficient probable cause in the affidavit.
impact: Affidavits must establish a clear nexus between suspected crime and location searched.

title: State v. Collins
summary: Inventory search upheld when conducted according to department policy.
impact: Following standardized inventory procedures protects evidence from suppression.

title: People v. Alvarez
summary: Anonymous tip alone was insufficient to establish probable cause without corroboration.
impact: Independent verification is required before acting on anonymous information.

title: U.S. v. Daniels
summary: Temporary detention was ruled lawful based on specific and articulable facts.
impact: Reasonable suspicion must be based on observable, documented behavior.

title: State v. Brooks
summary: Evidence suppressed after unlawful entry into a residence without exigent circumstances.
impact: Warrantless home entry requires clear exigency or consent.
